coredata.dc:title: Crown ether-like structure in graphene quantum dots: Ultra-sensitive photoluminescence sensor for Ca2+ in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yiwen
Zhou, Weilin
Wang, Ye
Zhang, Yiming
coredata.dc:description: Due to the weak coordination of alkaline−earth metal ions (such as Mg2+, Ca2+ and Sr2+), the design for corresponding fluorescent probes in vitro is much harder than those for heavy metal ions (such as Cu2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+). In this paper, the crown ether-like structure was introduced in graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs) via the hydrothermal treatment of alizarin. With the strong coordination of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs’ lattice, C-GQDs show much lower detection limit to Ca2+ (2 pM) than that of surface modified graphene quantum dots (100 pM). Moreover, the selectivity of C-GQDs can be controlled by the size modulation of crown ether-like structure. Precursors with different structures were carried out to modulate the size of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs. The obtained C-GQDs show good selectivity for Mg2+, Sr2+, and Ba2+, respectively. Finally, we also show the successful detection of Ca2+ in vitro based on C-GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent Probe
Crown ether-like structure
Photoluminescence
Alkaline−earth metal ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing mechanism and selectivity control of C-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Action Plan of Scientific and Technological Innovation for Sustainable Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from Action Plan of Scientific and Technological Innovation for Sustainable Development ( CKY2019-28 , Chongming District, Shanghai, China).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation process of C-GQDs.
(a) TEM image (b) size distribution histogram, (c) HR−TEM image, (d) HAADF HR−TEM image (O atoms were marked by red circles) and (e) AFM image of C-GQDs. (f) Raman spectrum of C-GQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C 1s spectrum and (c) O 1s spectrum of C-GQDs.

(a) normalized Uv−vis, PL and PLE spectra of C-GQDs aqueous solution. (b) Digital photograph for the C-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) under ultraviolet source. (c) The PL intensity change of C-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) under ultraviolet radiation (150 W mercury lamp with a wavelength of 320 nm). (d) The PL intensity change of C-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) under visible light irradiation. (e) The PL intensity change of C-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) with the present of different ions (Ca2+, Li+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, I−, Br- and CO3 2−). The concentration of ions is 10 nM. F and F0 is the PL intensity of C-GQDs aqueous solution with and without ions, respectively. (f) PL spectra of C-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) with different [Ca2+]. (g) PL intensity change of C-GQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) with the present of homocysteine. F and F0 is the PL intensity of C-GQDs aqueous solution with and without Ca2+, respectively.

(a) The change of τ of C-GQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) under different [Ca2+]. τ and τ0 is the PL lifetime of C-GQDs with and without homocysteine, respectively. (b) PL spectra of C-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) with 50 pM CaCl2 (black curve) and C-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) with 50 pM CaCl2 + 0.2 mM EDTA (red curve). (c) Precursors for C-GQDs-1, C-GQDs-2, and C-GQDs-3.
(a) Metabolic activity of hASCs cells hatched with different concentrations of C-GQDs. (b) Confocal photograph images of hASCs cells with and without Ca2+.
PL properties of C-GQDs, C-GQDs-1, C-GQDs-2, and C-GQDs-3.

head.ce:title: Crown ether-like structure in graphene quantum dots: Ultra-sensitive photoluminescence sensor for Ca2+ in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Stomatology, Shanghai Tenth People’s Hospital, Tongji University School of Medicine, 200072 Shanghai, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Stomatology, Shanghai Tenth People’s Hospital, Tongji University School of Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Stomatology, Shanghai Tenth People’s Hospital, School of Medicine, Tongji University, 200072, Shanghai, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the weak coordination of alkaline−earth metal ions (such as Mg2+, Ca2+ and Sr2+), the design for corresponding fluorescent probes in vitro is much harder than those for heavy metal ions (such as Cu2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+). In this paper, the crown ether-like structure was introduced in graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs) via the hydrothermal treatment of alizarin. With the strong coordination of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs’ lattice, C-GQDs show much lower detection limit to Ca2+ (2 pM) than that of surface modified graphene quantum dots (100 pM). Moreover, the selectivity of C-GQDs can be controlled by the size modulation of crown ether-like structure. Precursors with different structures were carried out to modulate the size of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs. The obtained C-GQDs show good selectivity for Mg2+, Sr2+, and Ba2+, respectively. Finally, we also show the successful detection of Ca2+ in vitro based on C-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Calcium ion (Ca2+) is an important metal ion in physiological processes [1]. Previous reports have demonstrated Ca2+ playing a crucial role in radiating nerve signals and osteogenesis process [2]. Human brains, for example, rely on electrical impulses to release calcium ions that bind to neurotransmitters that, in turn, communicate with the rest of the nervous system [3,4]. Neurons from patients with Alzheimer's also demonstrate compromised energy metabolism and calcium dysregulation [5,6]. Moreover, the concentration of free Ca2+ is an important indicator for the bone disease (such as osteoporosis) [7]. Thus, the development of in vitro/vivo Ca2+ detection techniques have great significance for the mechanism research, diagnosis and treat of nerve disease and bone disease. It's worth noting that, compared with other detection techniques, fluorescent bio-imaging and detection techniques is a sensitive, efficient and simple detection technique which show significant superiority [8]. However, due to the weak coordination of alkaline−earth metal ions (such as Mg2+, Ca2+ and Sr2+) [9], the design for corresponding fluorescent probes in vitro is much harder than those for heavy metal ions (such as Cu2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+) [10–12]. Above condition restrains the development of in vitro/vivo Ca2+ fluorescent bio-imaging and detection techniques.

Fortunately, as a new photoluminescent (PL) material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted wide interest due to the tunable PL performance, excellent biocompatibility and biological stability [13–15]. GQDs are zero-dimensional semiconductor graphene fragments. Due to the diversified surface functional groups (such as: C‒OH, C˭O, ‒COOH and ‒NH2) [14,16,17], the modification of GQDs is much easier than traditional PL materials (such as fluorescent proteins and fluorescent dyes) [18]. Moreover, the stable carbon based sp
2 framework result in the excellent biocompatibility and biological stability of GQDs which show significant advantage than traditional Ⅱ-Ⅵ quantum dots (such as CdS and CdTe) [19,20]. Due to above superiorities, GQDs have been demonstrated as a kind of biological materials with high application value [21,22].

Due to above urgency of development of in vitro/vivo Ca2+ detection techniques and the superiorities of GQDs, we demonstrate the successful structure design of GQDs. The crown ether-like structure was introduced in GQDs via the hydrothermal treatment of alizarin ( Fig. 1). The crown ether-like structure in obtained product (C-GQDs) was demonstrated by high-angle annular dark field high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HAADF HR−TEM) results. With the strong coordination of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs’ lattice, C-GQDs show much lower detection limit to Ca2+ (2 pM) than that of surface modified GQDs (100 pM). Moreover, the selectivity of C-GQDs can be controlled by the size modulation of crown ether-like structure. Precursors with different structures were carried out to modulate the size of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs. The obtained C-GQDs show good selectivity for Mg2+, Sr2+, and Ba2+, respectively. Finally, we also show the successful detection of Ca2+ in vitro based on C-GQDs.

All chemicals were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further purification. The typical preparation process of C-GQDs is as follows: 20 mg alizarin was dissolved in 10.0 mL deionized water. The solution transferred into a 15 mL Teflons-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 24 h. The obtained reaction solution was dialysed by 3500 Da dialysis bag for a weak. The yield of C-GQDs is 5.3 wt%.

For the structural characterization of C-GQDs, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was measured using H-8100 (Hitachi, Japan). Atomic force microscope (AFM, Cypher S, Asylum Research) was used to determine the height profiles of C-GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured using PHI Quantera II system (INC, Japan). For the optical property characterization of C-GQDs, the ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectrum was measured using UV-5800 spectrophotometer (Yuanxi, China). PL and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were measured using PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) under room temperature. Quinine sulfate [23,24] was selected to calculate the quantum yield (φ) of C-GQDs.

Fig. 2a shows the TEM image of obtained C-GQDs. Homogeneous dots with lateral size of 1–6 nm can be observed. The average lateral size is 2.7 nm (Fig. 2b). High resolution TEM (HR−TEM) image with atomic resolution is shown in Fig. 2c. The obvious lattice structure can be observed which indicates the crystallinity structure of C-GQDs. The interlayer spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the (1 1 2 0) lattice fringe of graphene [25–27]. Moreover, HAADF HR−TEM image (Fig. 2d) indicates the existence of O atoms on the edge of the nano-pore structures (0.4–2 nm) which demonstrated the crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs. The crown ether-like structure can be due to the condensation reaction of alizarin under solvothermal reaction environment (Fig. 1) [27]. AFM image of highly-dispersed C-GQDs on a 300 nm SiO2/Si substrate can be observed in Fig. 2e. The thicknesses of C-GQDs are 0.4–0.8 nm, which corresponds to 1–2 graphite layers [28,29]. Fig. 2f shows the Raman spectra of these C-GQDs. The defect-related D and G bands can be observed at 1350 and 1580 cm−1, respectively [30,31]. The peak intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) is higher (ID/IG = 0.72) than that of other GQDs (ID/IG = 0.2–0.4) which can be due to the nano-pore structures in C-GQDs [32,33].

The surface chemical structure of C-GQDs is characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3a, the peak located at ca. 284.2 eV and ca. 532 eV in XPS survey spectrum of C-GQDs can be attributed to the C 1s and O 1s signals [34,35]. The O content of C-GQDs is 8.3 at%. The well-fitted C 1s spectrum of C-GQDs (Fig. 3b) indicates the existence of C‒C/C˭C (284.7 eV), C‒O‒C (285.3 eV) and C˭O (288.3 eV) groups [36,37]. The well-fitted O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) indicates the existence of C‒O‒C (531.4 eV) and C˭O (532.9 eV) groups correspondingly [22,38]. It's worth noting that most O (78%) exist in the form of C‒O‒C groups which can be due to the crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs. Moreover, the oxygen-containing functional groups result in the excellent water dispersibility of C-GQDs [39,40]. Indeed, the Zeta potential of C-GQDs is −44 mV.

Optical properties of C-GQDs were represented via Uv−vis, PL and PLE spectra. Fig. 4a shows the Uv−vis spectrum of C-GQDs. The absorption peak located at 210 nm can be attributed to the π−π* transition of the sp
2 domains in C-GQDs [41,42]. On the other hand, the peak located at 330 nm can be attributed to the n−π* transition of O atoms in C-GQDs [43,44]. The optimal excitation wavelength (λex) and emission wavelength (λem) of C-GQDs is 408 and 495 nm, respectively which can be observed in the PLE and PL spectra in Fig. 4a. The quantum yield of C-GQDs is 0.74 which is higher than that of most reported GQDs [11–15,18,19,21,23,24,26–29,31–36,38,41–43,45–50]. Indeed, the C-GQDs aqueous solution emits bright green PL under ultraviolet source (as shown in Fig. 4b).

Due to the stable C‒O‒C groups in C-GQDs, these C-GQDs show good photostability. As shown in Fig. 4c−d, C-GQDs show no obvious decrease in PL intensity under the 72 h UV light irradiation (Xe lamp with the center wavelength of 320 nm, Fig. 4c) or 12 days visible light irradiation (450–850 nm, Fig. 4d). Fig. 4e shows the PL intensity change of 0.1 mg mL−1 C-GQDs aqueous solution with different ions. No obvious change of PL intensity can be observed with the present of Li+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, I−, Br− and CO3 2−. However, strong quenching effect can be observed with the present of Ca2+. The PL intensity of C-GQDs follows the equation as follows [47]:

where [Ca2+] is the Ca2+ concentration. F0 and F are PL intensities of C-GQDs without and with the presence of Ca2+, respectively. The PL intensity of C-GQDs shows good linear dependence with [Ca2+] in the range of 0–200 pM (Fig. 4f−g). The detection limit of C-GQDs is 2 pM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 which is much lower than most reported PL sensors [50–53]. Moreover, C-GQDs also show no obvious quenching with the present of high concentrated K+ and Na+ (Figure S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism and selectivity control of C-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PL response of C-GQDs can be due to the coordination between Ca2+ and crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs [9]. Lifetime (τ) measurements of C-CQDs with different [Ca2+] were carried out for further evidence of quenching mechanism. In static quenching (mainly due to the coordination process), fluorescence lifetime does not change (τ0/τ = 1) [46,48]. Otherwise, in dynamic quenching, F0/F = τ0/τ. Fig. 5a shows the effect of [Ca2+] on the τ of C-CQDs. The τ of C-CQDs did not change (τ0/τ ≈ 1) upon Ca2+ addition. This indicates the quenching of C-GQDs can be due to the static quenching process. Indeed, the PL recovery of C-CQDs can be observed when adding a strong Ca2+ chelator (such as: EDTA, Fig. 5b).

Previous work reported polyethylene glycol surface modified GQDs which also can be used in the PL detection of Ca2+ [9]. However, the surface modified structure result in the low quenching efficiency. By contrast, due to the strong coordination of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs’ lattice, C-GQDs show much lower detection limit.

Moreover, the selectivity of C-GQDs can be controlled by the size modulation of crown ether-like structure. Precursors with different structures were carried out to modulate the size of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs (Fig. 5c). Due to the different chemical structure of precursors (site of ‒OH groups/size of aromatic nucleus), the polymerization process of precursors was different. With the larger aromatic nucleus size, the obtained C-GQDs (such as C-GQDs-3) show much larger size of crown ether-like structure than that in other C-GQDs. On the other hand, due to the different site of ‒OH groups of precursors, the size of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs also can be modulated. As shown in Table 1, C-GQDs-1, C-GQDs-2, and C-GQDs-3 show good selectivity for Mg2+, Sr2+, and Ba2+, respectively. The detection limit of C-GQDs-1, C-GQDs-2, and C-GQDs-3 is 20 pM, 8 pM, and 2 pM, respectively.

Above results demonstrated the excellent detectability for Ca2+ of C-GQDs which indicates the potential application value of C-GQDs in Ca2+ detection in vitro. Finally, we show the successful detection of Ca2+ in vitro. The cytotoxicity of C-GQDs is evaluated by human adipose-derived stem cells (hASCs) for practical application [45,49]]. No obvious change in cell viability (above 96% after 24 h, 600 μg mL−1 C-GQDs) for hASCs cells ( Fig. 6a) can be observed after C-GQDs (0–600 μg mL−1) treatment. This indicates the C-GQDs show low cytotoxicity in vitro and can be used in practical application. Fig. 5b shows the confocal fluorescence microphotograph of hASCs after the introduce of C-GQDs. The hASCs cells stained with C-GQDs (green). The nucleus was marked by 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI). Bright green PL can be observed in hASCs after the introduce of C-GQDs. Granular C-GQDs can be observed in cytoplasm. However, no obvious PL signal can be observed in above system after the introduce of Ca2+ (CaCl2, 20 nM).

In summary, we demonstrated the ultra-sensitive photoluminescence sensing ability for Ca2+ of C-GQDs. With the strong coordination of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs’ lattice, C-GQDs show much lower detection limit to Ca2+ (2 pM) than that of surface modified GQDs (100 pM). Moreover, the selectivity of C-GQDs can be controlled by the size modulation of crown ether-like structure. Precursors with different structures were carried out to modulate the size of crown ether-like structure in C-GQDs. The obtained C-GQDs show good selectivity for Mg2+, Sr2+, and Ba2+, respectively. Finally, we also show the successful detection of Ca2+ in vitro based on C-GQDs. This work is helpful for the development of carbon based fluorescent probes for ions with weak coordination ability.

Yiwen Huang: performed the experiments, data analysis, wrote the manuscript. Weilin Zhou: Data curation, wrote the manuscript. Ye Wang: discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript. Yiming Zhang: designed and supervised the overall project.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from Action Plan of Scientific and Technological Innovation for Sustainable Development (CKY2019-28, Chongming District, Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.synthmet.2020.116581.
